content.full_text:
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
Technical Assistance 
(916) 322·5662 
Michael K. Zweig 
Sacks & Zweig 
Administration 
322.5660 
9255 Sunset Blvd., Suite 620 
Los Angeles, 
CA 
90069 
Dear Mr. Zweig: 
Executive/legal 
•• 
Enfarcement 
32~·5901 
322·6441 
October 21, 1983 
Statements of Economic Interest 
322·6444 
This is in reply to your letter dated October 3, 1983, in 
which you asked us to p~ovide advice regarding a hypothetical set 
of facts. 
Because you did not ask these questions on behalf of a 
specifically identified person, this letter does not constitute 
advice under Gov. Code Section 83114. 
(See 2 Cal. Adm. Code 
Section 18329(b).) 
Rather, this letter addresses the questions in 
general terms in order to provide interpretive guidelines. 
You presented a hypothetical situation in which Mr. Smith asks 
Mr. Jones to make a contribution to a committee. 
Mr. Smith in no 
way implies that he will in any way reimburse Mr. Jones for the 
contribution; Mr. Smith does not reimburse Mr. Jones, nor does Mr. 
Smith forgive any indebtedness, or in any other way make Mr. Jones 
whole for the contribution that Mr. Jones made to the committee. 
Based on this hypothetical factual situation, following are answers 
to each of your questions: 
1. 
Is Mr. Jones an "intermediary" for Mr. Smith as that term 
is used in Gov. Code Section 84302? 
No, Mr. Jones is not an "intermediary." 
The mere suggestion 
by one person that another person make a contribution does not 
create an "intermediary" situation. 
FPPC advice letter No. A-79-77 
(Dec. 13, 1979) provides a functional definition of the term 
"intermediary" which is consistent with the disclosure purposes of 
the Political Reform Act. 
A copy of that letter is attached for 
your information. 
2. 
Is Mr. Jones an "agent" of Mr. Smith as that term is used 
in Gov. Code Section 84302? 
No, Mr. Jones is not an "agent" of Mr. Smith. 
The same 
reasoning as indicated above to question number 1 applies here. 
The term "agent" as used in the campaign disclosure provisions is 
applicable to a person receiving a contribution on behalf of a 


Michael K. Zweig 
October 21, 1983 
Page 2 
candidate or committee and to a person making an expenditure on 
behalf of a candidate or committee, and not necessarily to a person 
making a contribution on behalf of another. 
With regard to making 
a contribution, an "agent" would be a person who merely delivers or 
transmits a contribution when it is clear from the check or other 
written instrument who the source of the contribution is. 
A person 
acting as an "agent" is not required to be reported on a campaign 
disclosure statement. 
3. 
Has Mr. Jones made a "contribution on behalf of" Mr. Smith 
as that phrase is used in Gov. Code Section 84302? 
No, because the funds are Mr. Jones': not Mr. Smith's. 
4. 
Regarding the facts stated in the hypothetical, have any 
of Mr. Smith, Mr. Jones or the committee failed to report anything 
which the 1974 Political Reform Act or other California law 
required them to report? 
Under the facts of the hypothetical, the funds used to make 
the contribution are solely Mr. Jones', and Mr. Jones will not in 
any way be reimbursed or compensated; therefore, Mr. Jones is the 
actual source of the contribution, and he is the only person who 
must be disclosed on the committee's campaign disclosure statement. 
I hope this response provides some guidance to you in 
understanding the campaign disclosure provisions of the Political 
Reform Act. 
Please call me if you have any questions. 
JP:kt

sections.question: You presented a hypothetical situation in which Mr. Smith asks Mr. Jones to make a contribution to a committee. Mr. Smith in no way implies that he will in any way reimburse Mr. Jones for the contribution; Mr. Smith does not reimburse Mr. Jones, nor does Mr. Smith forgive any indebtedness, or in any other way make Mr. Jones whole for the contribution that Mr. Jones made to the committee. Based on this hypothetical factual situation, following are answers to each of your questions: 1. Is Mr. Jones an "intermediary" for Mr. Smith as that term is used in Gov. Code Section 84302? 2. Is Mr. Jones an "agent" of Mr. Smith as that term is used in Gov. Code Section 84302? 3. Has Mr. Jones made a "contribution on behalf of" Mr. Smith as that phrase is used in Gov. Code Section 84302? 4. Regarding the facts stated in the hypothetical, have any of Mr. Smith, Mr. Jones or the committee failed to report anything which the 1974 Political Reform Act or other California law required them to report?
sections.conclusion: No, Mr. Jones is not an "intermediary" or "agent" of Mr. Smith. The mere suggestion by one person that another person make a contribution does not create an intermediary situation. An agent making a contribution would be a person who merely delivers or transmits a contribution when the source is clear from written instrument. Under the facts, the funds are solely Mr. Jones', Mr. Jones will not be reimbursed or compensated, therefore Mr. Jones is the actual source of the contribution and is the only person who must be disclosed on the committee's campaign disclosure statement.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether Mr. Jones qualifies as an intermediary or agent of Mr. Smith under campaign finance law when Mr. Smith requests that Mr. Jones make a contribution to a committee without reimbursement, and whether any reporting obligations are triggered under these circumstances.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concludes that Mr. Jones is neither an intermediary nor agent of Mr. Smith, as the mere request to contribute without reimbursement does not establish such relationships. Mr. Jones, as the actual source of the funds, is the only person required to be disclosed on the campaign disclosure statement.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains four numbered questions with corresponding answers. FPPC notes this is interpretive guidance rather than formal advice under Gov. Code Section 83114 because the inquiry was hypothetical and not on behalf of a specifically identified person. Letter references FPPC advice letter A-79-77 for definition of intermediary.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: You presented a hypothetical situation in which Mr. Smith asks Mr. Jones to make a contribution to a committee. Mr. Smith in no way implies that he will in any way reimburse Mr. Jones for the contribution; Mr. Smith does not reimburse Mr. Jones, nor does Mr. Smith forgive any indebtedness, or in any other way make Mr. Jones whole for the contribution that Mr. Jones made to the committee. Based on this hypothetical factual situation, following are answers to each of your questions: 1. Is Mr. Jones an "intermediary" for Mr. Smith as that term is used in Gov. Code Section 84302? 2. Is Mr. Jones an "agent" of Mr. Smith as that term is used in Gov. Code Section 84302? 3. Has Mr. Jones made a "contribution on behalf of" Mr. Smith as that phrase is used in Gov. Code Section 84302? 4. Regarding the facts stated in the hypothetical, have any of Mr. Smith, Mr. Jones or the committee failed to report anything which the 1974 Political Reform Act or other California law required them to report?

CONCLUSION: No, Mr. Jones is not an "intermediary" or "agent" of Mr. Smith. The mere suggestion by one person that another person make a contribution does not create an intermediary situation. An agent making a contribution would be a person who merely delivers or transmits a contribution when the source is clear from written instrument. Under the facts, the funds are solely Mr. Jones', Mr. Jones will not be reimbursed or compensated, therefore Mr. Jones is the actual source of the contribution and is the only person who must be disclosed on the committee's campaign disclosure statement.

embedding.summary: FPPC provides interpretive guidance on a hypothetical scenario involving campaign contributions, clarifying that a person who makes a contribution at another's request without reimbursement is not an intermediary or agent, but rather the actual source of the contribution requiring disclosure.
